questions.text: In the 1950s, two programs ran one after the other on the same machine. What stopped one program from reading the other's data?

options.A: Nothing — there was no protection between programs at all
options.B: The human operator removed each program from memory before the next ran
options.C: Programs were each given separate physical sections of the machine
options.D: Programs were not allowed to store data in memory at all

questions.explanation:
In the 1950s, the OS was just a library — no hardware memory protection existed.

```
Program A runs → operator removes it → Program B runs
```

Both A and B are valid answers:
- **(A)** At the hardware level, truly nothing stopped a program from reading any address
- **(B)** In practice, the operator loaded one program at a time, so they never overlapped

The "protection" was sequential operation + human oversight, not any technical mechanism.

questions.text: In batch processing, jobs were run one by one by a human operator. What was the main problem for users?

options.A: Users had to wait hours or days to see their program results
options.B: Programs could not be saved and had to be resubmitted every day
options.C: The operator could only run programs written by the same company
options.D: Only programs shorter than one punch card could be submitted

questions.explanation:
Batch processing automated job execution, but users still paid a steep price in **wait time**.

```
Submit punch cards → batch collected → run overnight → results next day
```

The user experience: submit a job Friday, find a typo in the output Monday.
This pain is what motivated **time-sharing** — giving each user a fast, interactive slice of the machine.

questions.text: Which statements about multiprogramming are correct?

options.A: Each program in memory gets its own dedicated CPU permanently
options.B: Programs run faster because they can share each other's variables
options.C: When one program waits for I/O, another program can use the CPU
options.D: Keeping several programs in memory reduces wasted CPU idle time

questions.explanation:
Multiprogramming keeps several programs in memory and switches between them when one blocks on I/O.

**C ✓** — When Program 1 waits for disk, the CPU switches to Program 2. I/O overlap.
**D ✓** — With multiple programs loaded, the CPU is rarely idle.

**A ✗** — There's only one CPU. Programs share it; none gets it permanently.
**B ✗** — Programs have isolated memory. They cannot touch each other's variables.

questions.text: Linux became popular in the 1990s mainly because it was:

options.A: faster than all other operating systems available at the time
options.B: developed by a large company with strong legal protection
options.C: free, open source, and built on proven UNIX design principles
options.D: the only OS that could run on the new personal computers

questions.explanation:
Linux was created by Linus Torvalds in 1991 as a free, open-source alternative to expensive UNIX systems.

Three reasons for its success:
1. **Free** — zero licensing cost
2. **Open source** — full source code visible and modifiable
3. **UNIX design** — decades of battle-tested principles

It wasn't the fastest OS, Torvalds was a student (not a big company), and Windows already ran on PCs.

questions.text: `counter = 10`. Thread A loads 10. Before A stores, Thread B also loads 10. Both add 1. Both store. What is the final value of `counter`?

options.A: 12 — both threads each added 1 successfully to the value
options.B: 11 — one increment was lost because both threads read the same value
options.C: 10 — the two stores overwrote each other and cancelled out
options.D: 20 — each thread doubled the value before storing its result

questions.explanation:
A classic **race condition** — the result depends on which thread runs first.

```
Thread A:  LOAD counter (gets 10)
Thread B:  LOAD counter (gets 10)   ← reads before A stores!
Thread A:  ADD 1 → 11,  STORE 11
Thread B:  ADD 1 → 11,  STORE 11
                        ↑
                   should be 12, but one increment is lost
```

The fix is a **lock** — making the load/add/store sequence atomic (indivisible).

questions.text: Which is an example of a scheduling **policy**, not a mechanism?

options.A: Saving all CPU registers into the process control block
options.B: Switching the CPU from user mode to kernel mode on a trap
options.C: Jumping to the OS interrupt handler when the timer fires
options.D: Running the process that has been waiting the longest first

questions.explanation:
There is a sharp line between **mechanism** (how) and **policy** (what/which).

- **Mechanism** → the implementation: saving registers, switching CPU modes
- **Policy** → the decision: which process runs next?

**(D)** "Run the process that has waited longest" is a *decision rule* → **policy**

A, B, C all describe low-level implementation procedures → **mechanisms**

questions.text: Which are main OS responsibilities?

options.A: Virtualization, making physical resources appear as many virtual ones
options.B: Compilation, turning source code into executable binary files
options.C: Persistence, keeping data safe even when power is lost
options.D: Defragmentation, reorganizing disk files to improve read speed

questions.explanation:
The OS has three core responsibilities: **Virtualization**, **Concurrency**, **Persistence**.

**A ✓** — Virtualization: making one CPU/memory appear as many private resources
**C ✓** — Persistence: keeping data safe through power loss (file systems, journaling)

**B ✗** — Compilation is done by the *compiler* (gcc, clang), not the OS
**D ✗** — Defragmentation is a storage utility, not a core OS responsibility

questions.text: A database writes records to disk. The computer crashes halfway through. On restart, some records are missing. Which OS responsibility failed?

options.A: Concurrency — two threads wrote to the database at the same time
options.B: Resource Management — the CPU was not shared correctly between tasks
options.C: Persistence — the write was not completed and made safe on disk
options.D: Virtualization — memory addresses were mapped to the wrong location

questions.explanation:
Records lost after a crash mid-write = a **Persistence** failure.

```
DB writes record 1 ✓
DB writes record 2 ✓
POWER LOSS
DB writes record 3 ✗ ← lost
```

The file system is responsible for **crash consistency** — ensuring that even a mid-write power failure leaves the disk in a safe state. Tools: journaling, copy-on-write, `fsync()`.

questions.text: Program X stores a value at virtual address `0x2000`. Program Y also has a variable at virtual address `0x2000`. Program X changes its value. What happens to Program Y's variable?

options.A: Y still holds its original value — each address maps to a different physical location
options.B: Y also changes — both programs share the same physical memory cell
options.C: An error occurs — two programs cannot use the same virtual address
options.D: Y is set to zero — the OS protects Y by zeroing the shared cell

questions.explanation:
Every process has its **own private virtual address space**.

```
Process X           Process Y
vaddr 0x2000        vaddr 0x2000
     │                   │
     ▼                   ▼
physical 0x8000     physical 0xC000   ← different locations!
```

The OS (via the MMU) keeps separate page tables per process. X writing to 0x2000 touches physical 0x8000 — Y's 0x2000 at physical 0xC000 is completely unaffected.

questions.text: What is the correct term for giving each process a small slice of CPU time, switching quickly between them?

options.A: Space sharing
options.B: Batch processing
options.C: Time sharing
options.D: Multiprogramming

questions.explanation:
The CPU can only run one thing at a time — simultaneous appearance is an **illusion**.

```
time ──────────────────────────────────────►
  P1  ████░░░░████░░░░████░░░░
  P2  ░░░░████░░░░████░░░░████
  P3  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  (waiting)
      ← 10ms →
```

The OS switches fast enough (~100–1000×/sec) that humans perceive smooth parallelism. This is **time sharing** — CPU virtualization.

questions.text: Which statements about kernel mode and user mode are correct?

options.A: In kernel mode, the OS can access any memory or hardware device
options.B: In user mode, programs must use system calls to request OS services
options.C: In kernel mode, the OS executes with the same limits as user mode
options.D: In user mode, programs run faster because fewer checks are performed

questions.explanation:
The CPU hardware enforces two privilege levels.

```
┌─────────────────────────────┐
│        KERNEL MODE          │  full access: any memory, any instruction
├─────────────────────────────┤
│         USER MODE           │  restricted: must use system calls
└─────────────────────────────┘
```

**A ✓** — Kernel mode: unrestricted access to all memory and hardware
**B ✓** — User mode: programs must go through system calls for OS services

**C ✗** — Kernel mode has *fewer* restrictions, not the same as user mode
**D ✗** — User mode is not "faster" — the restrictions exist for safety, not speed

questions.text: Why does the OS run in kernel mode while user programs run in user mode?

options.A: Kernel mode makes the OS code execute at a faster clock speed
options.B: User mode prevents programs from directly accessing hardware or other programs' memory
options.C: Kernel mode allows the OS to use more RAM than user programs can
options.D: User mode programs cannot create new processes without switching modes

questions.explanation:
User mode exists to **protect** the system from programs — not to limit speed.

Without user mode, any program could:
- Overwrite OS kernel data structures
- Read another process's memory (passwords, keys)
- Crash the entire machine

User mode is **hardware enforcement** of least privilege.
Programs must ask the OS via **system calls** to do anything privileged.

questions.text: What is the difference between a **program** and a **process**?

options.A: A program is temporary; a process is stored permanently on disk
options.B: A program runs in kernel mode; a process runs only in user mode
options.C: A program uses memory; a process is a file waiting to be loaded
options.D: A program is a file on disk; a process is that program actively running

questions.explanation:
```
Program                    Process
───────                    ───────
File on disk          →    Running in memory
Static / passive           Active / dynamic
Code + data                Code + data + stack + heap + PC + registers
One copy                   Many instances possible
```

**(D)** A program is the file; a process is that file actively executing.

questions.text: You run the same Python script 4 times at the same time. How many programs and processes are there?

options.A: 1 program and 4 processes
options.B: 4 programs and 4 processes
options.C: 4 programs and 1 process
options.D: 1 program and 1 process

questions.explanation:
One file, four instances:

```
python script.py  →  Process PID 101  (own stack, heap, registers)
python script.py  →  Process PID 102  (own stack, heap, registers)
python script.py  →  Process PID 103  (own stack, heap, registers)
python script.py  →  Process PID 104  (own stack, heap, registers)
     ▲
  1 program file on disk
```

**1 program, 4 processes.** Each is independent — they can't access each other's variables.

questions.text:
A process has `int counter = 0;` declared at the top of the file, outside any function. Where is this stored in the address space?

```
HIGH ADDRESS
┌──────────────┐
│    Stack     │
│    (free)    │
│    Heap      │
├──────────────┤
│  Static Data │
├──────────────┤
│     Code     │
└──────────────┘
LOW ADDRESS
```

options.A: Stack — it is used inside function calls at runtime
options.B: Heap — it can grow and shrink while the program runs
options.C: Static Data — it is a global variable with a fixed location
options.D: Code — it is part of the compiled binary instructions

questions.explanation:
```
HIGH ADDRESS
┌──────────────┐
│    Stack     │  ← local variables, function call frames
│    (free)    │
│    Heap      │  ← malloc / new  (dynamic allocation)
├──────────────┤
│  Static Data │  ← global variables  ← int counter = 0 lives here
├──────────────┤
│     Code     │  ← compiled instructions
└──────────────┘
LOW ADDRESS
```

`int counter = 0` declared *outside any function* is a **global variable** → **Static Data**. It has a fixed address that exists for the entire program lifetime.

questions.text:
Why does P0 change from READY to RUNNING at time 5?

```
Time | P0        | P1
  1  | READY     | RUNNING
  2  | READY     | RUNNING
  3  | READY     | RUNNING   ← P1 sends data over the network
  4  | READY     | BLOCKED
  5  | RUNNING   | BLOCKED
```

options.A: P0 has a higher scheduling priority than P1 in this system
options.B: A timer fired at time 4 and the OS randomly selected P0
options.C: P0 requested more memory and was rewarded with CPU time
options.D: P1 is waiting for network I/O, so the OS runs P0 instead

questions.explanation:
```
Time | P0        | P1
  1  | READY     | RUNNING
  2  | READY     | RUNNING
  3  | READY     | RUNNING   ← P1 calls send() over network
  4  | READY     | BLOCKED   ← waiting for network hardware
  5  | RUNNING   | BLOCKED   ← CPU is free → OS runs P0
```

P1 initiated a **slow I/O operation** (network send). The OS immediately moved it to BLOCKED and gave the CPU to the next READY process. No timer, no priority — just I/O-driven switching.

questions.text: Which items are stored in the Process Control Block (PCB)?

options.A: The process ID (PID) and the ID of its parent process
options.B: The complete program binary loaded from disk at startup
options.C: A log of every system call the process has made so far
options.D: Information about which files and devices the process has open

questions.explanation:
The **PCB** is the OS's complete record for each process — stored in kernel memory.

**A ✓** — PID and parent PID: fundamental identifiers for process trees
**D ✓** — Open file descriptors and devices: tracked so the OS can clean up on exit

**B ✗** — The binary lives on disk (memory-mapped), not in the PCB
**C ✗** — System call logs are not stored per-process in the PCB

options.A: only "hello" — the parent process exits right after fork()
options.B: only "world" — the child process never reaches the output line
options.C: "hello" then "world"
options.D: "world" then "hello"

questions.explanation:
`fork()` creates a child — both parent and child continue from the same point.

```
        fork()
       /      \
  Parent        Child
  rc > 0        rc = 0
  prints        prints
  "world"       "hello"
```

The OS scheduler can run either process first → **both orderings are valid**:
- **(C)** "hello" then "world"
- **(D)** "world" then "hello"

A and B are wrong: both processes *do* reach their print statements — neither exits right after fork.

questions.text: A child process was just created by fork(). What value does the **child** receive as the return value of fork()?

options.A: 0 — the child always receives zero from fork()
options.B: Its own PID — so it knows its own process identity
options.C: The parent's PID — so it knows who created it
options.D: -1 — the value that signals success in UNIX system calls

questions.explanation:
`fork()` returns a different value depending on who you are:

```
┌────────┬──────────────────────────────┐
│ Who    │ Return value                 │
├────────┼──────────────────────────────┤
│ Parent │ Child's PID  (e.g., 4821)    │
│ Child  │ 0                            │
│ Error  │ -1                           │
└────────┴──────────────────────────────┘
```

The child gets `0` — a signal meaning "you are the newly created child." The standard pattern: `if (rc == 0) { /* I am the child */ }`

questions.text: What is the main weakness of cooperative scheduling?

options.A: It switches between programs too often, causing too much overhead
options.B: It requires a hardware timer that not all computers support
options.C: A program that never calls a system call can run forever, blocking others
options.D: Programs must be written in a special way to give up the CPU

questions.text:
A timer fires while Process A is running. Which sequence is correct?

```
1: CPU switches to kernel mode and jumps to the interrupt handler
2: OS saves A's kernel registers into A's process structure
3: Timer interrupt fires while A is running
4: OS loads B's registers from B's process structure and jumps to B
5: Hardware saves A's user-level registers onto the kernel stack
```
questions.explanation:
Two phases: **hardware** saves first, then **OS software** saves.

```
(3) Timer fires
     │
     ▼
(5) Hardware: saves user registers → kernel stack   [automatic]
     │
     ▼
(1) CPU → kernel mode, jumps to interrupt handler
     │
     ▼
(2) OS software: saves kernel registers → PCB       [explicit code]
     │
     ▼
(4) OS: loads new process registers → runs it
```

Correct order: **3 → 5 → 1 → 2 → 4**

questions.text: When a timer interrupt fires, the hardware saves the current process's registers. Where are they saved?

options.A: Into the process control block (PCB) in main memory
options.B: Into a special register file inside the CPU core
options.C: Into the kernel stack for the current process
options.D: Into a region of the user process's own stack memory

questions.explanation:
Two saves happen during a context switch — **hardware** does the first:

```
Timer fires
    │
    ▼  [hardware, automatic]
User registers ──────────────► Kernel stack
    │
    ▼  [OS software, explicit]
Kernel registers ────────────► PCB
```

When the interrupt fires, the CPU hardware automatically saves the running process's registers onto that process's **kernel stack** before any OS code runs. The OS then copies from there to the PCB.

questions.text: Which are hidden costs of a context switch?

options.A: The process must be recompiled before it can run again
options.B: Memory used by the old process must be freed immediately
options.C: The CPU pipeline is flushed, wasting partially processed instructions
options.D: The CPU cache holds data from the old process, causing misses for the new one

questions.explanation:
Context switches have **hidden** performance costs beyond register save/restore:

**C ✓** — **Pipeline flush**: the CPU was mid-way through executing instructions. Switching discards that partially-processed work.

**D ✓** — **Cache pollution**: the CPU cache was warm for the old process. The new process accesses different memory → cache misses → slow until the cache refills.

A and B are false: code doesn't reload from disk, and memory isn't freed on a switch.

questions.text: Why must only the OS kernel be allowed to control the hardware timer?

options.A: Timer hardware requires kernel-level assembly code to program correctly
options.B: Two programs sharing timer access would cause the timer to lose accuracy
options.C: A program that stops the timer could take over the CPU and never be interrupted
options.D: The timer is the only mechanism the OS has to forcibly regain control of the CPU

questions.explanation:
The timer is the OS's **only weapon** for preemptive control.

```
Timer disabled by user program
        │
        ▼
OS never gets an interrupt
        │
        ▼
Program runs forever — machine taken hostage
```

**C ✓** — Stopping the timer = program runs forever uninterrupted
**D ✓** — The timer interrupt is the *only* mechanism the OS has to forcibly reclaim the CPU

This is why timer control is a **privileged operation** — kernel mode only.

questions.text: Response time is defined as:

options.A: T_completion − T_arrival
options.B: T_firstrun − T_arrival
options.C: T_completion − T_firstrun
options.D: T_arrival − T_firstrun

questions.explanation:
Two key scheduling metrics:

```
arrival          first run         completion
   │                 │                  │
   └── response ─────┘                  │
   │                                    │
   └──────────── turnaround ────────────┘
```

**Response time** = T_firstrun − T_arrival → how quickly does the system react?
**Turnaround time** = T_completion − T_arrival → how long from submission to done?

**(B)** Response time = T_firstrun − T_arrival ✓

questions.text: [Calculation] Three jobs arrive at time 0: A=20s, B=20s, C=20s. FIFO: A→B→C. Average turnaround time?
questions.explanation:
FIFO with equal jobs — straightforward calculation:

```
|── A (20s) ──|── B (20s) ──|── C (20s) ──|
0            20            40            60
```

| Job | Finish | Turnaround |
|-----|--------|-----------|
| A   | 20     | 20s       |
| B   | 40     | 40s       |
| C   | 60     | 60s       |

Average = (20 + 40 + 60) / 3 = **40s**

questions.text: [Calculation] Three jobs arrive at time 0: A=90s, B=5s, C=5s. FIFO: A→B→C. Average turnaround time?
questions.explanation:
The **convoy effect** — one long job delays everything behind it:

```
|──────────── A (90s) ────────────|─ B ─|─ C ─|
0                                90    95   100
```

| Job | Finish | Turnaround |
|-----|--------|-----------|
| A   | 90     | 90s       |
| B   | 95     | 95s       |
| C   | 100    | 100s      |

Average = (90 + 95 + 100) / 3 = **95s**

B and C are 5s of work but spend 90s waiting. SJF (B→C→A) would give ≈ 38s average.

questions.text: [Calculation] Three jobs arrive at time 0: A=8s, B=3s, C=12s. SJF (shortest first). What is the **response time** for job A?

options.A: 3s
options.B: 8s
options.C: 0s

questions.explanation:
SJF orders by length: B(3s) → A(8s) → C(12s)

```
|─ B ─|──── A ────|──────── C ────────|
0     3          11                  23
```

**Response time for A** = time A first runs − time A arrived = **3 − 0 = 3s**

A's first run starts at t=3 (after B finishes). It arrived at t=0.

questions.text: [Calculation] Round Robin, time slice = 2s, 5 jobs all arriving at time 0. Worst-case response time?

options.A: 2s
options.B: 4s
options.D: 8s

questions.explanation:
RR worst-case response time = **(N − 1) × time_slice**

```
t=0:  Job 1 ██  (response = 0s)
t=2:  Job 2 ██  (response = 2s)
t=4:  Job 3 ██  (response = 4s)
t=6:  Job 4 ██  (response = 6s)
t=8:  Job 5 ██  (response = 8s)  ← worst case
```

(5 − 1) × 2s = **8s** — the last job must wait for all others to take one slice first.

questions.text: A student says: "SJF is the best scheduler so we should always use it." What is wrong with this?

options.A: SJF requires too much memory to keep all jobs sorted by length
options.B: SJF can only handle a maximum of 10 jobs at once
options.C: The OS has no way of knowing in advance how long each job will run
options.D: SJF only works correctly on computers with multiple CPU cores

questions.explanation:
SJF is optimal in theory, **impossible in practice** — because:

```
Job arrives  →  How long will it run?
                        ↑
                 UNKNOWN until it finishes
```

The OS cannot know if a job will run for 1ms or 10 minutes before starting it. Job length depends on user input, data, branching — all unknown at submission time.

Not a memory problem, not a hardware problem — a fundamental **information problem**.

questions.text: Which statements about Round Robin are correct?

options.A: Round Robin gives each job a fast first turn, improving response time
options.B: Round Robin gives very low average turnaround time for most workloads
options.C: Round Robin shares the CPU fairly by giving each job a fixed time slice
options.D: Round Robin must know each job's total run time before it can start

questions.explanation:
Round Robin trades turnaround for response time.

**A ✓** — Every job gets its first turn within (N−1) × slice → **fast response time**
**C ✓** — CPU shared via a **fixed time slice** — RR's defining mechanism

**B ✗** — RR has *poor* average turnaround: it stretches all jobs out equally
**D ✗** — RR needs *no* foreknowledge of job length — it just rotates blindly

questions.text: A job uses 8ms of CPU, then does I/O. Allotment per level = 20ms. After I/O, what is its priority?

options.A: Reduced — the job used some CPU time, even under the allotment
options.B: Moved to lowest — all jobs that do I/O are moved to the bottom
options.C: Reset to top — finishing I/O always triggers a full priority reset
options.D: Unchanged — the job has not used up its full 20ms allotment yet

questions.explanation:
MLFQ tracks allotment usage cumulatively — 8ms used out of 20ms means **12ms remain**.

```
Q2  [████████░░░░░░░░░░░░]  8ms used, 12ms left
         ↑
    returns from I/O here → stays at Q2 with 12ms remaining
```

**(D) Unchanged** — the job hasn't exhausted its allotment yet, so MLFQ doesn't demote it.

I/O-intensive jobs naturally stay at high priority because they never burn through their full allotment.

questions.text: A CPU-bound job enters MLFQ. Q2 (high) → Q1 → Q0 (low). Allotment = 15ms. No I/O. After 45ms, which queue?

options.A: Q0 — demoted twice: Q2→Q1, then Q1→Q0
options.B: Q1 — demoted once after using Q2's allotment
options.C: Q1 — reaching Q0 requires more than 30ms of CPU use
options.D: Q2 — using the CPU alone does not change a job's priority

questions.explanation:
CPU-bound job, allotment = 15ms per level, no I/O:

```
 Q2  [████████████████] 15ms → DEMOTE
 Q1  [████████████████] 15ms → DEMOTE
 Q0  ← sitting here after 45ms
```

After 3 × 15ms = 45ms, demoted twice. Now at **Q0**.

MLFQ learns from behavior: a job that keeps burning its full allotment is CPU-bound and belongs at the bottom.

questions.text: If MLFQ never uses priority boost, what happens to a CPU-bound job at Q0?

options.A: It gets moved to a higher queue after it has waited long enough
options.B: It keeps running but slower, since Q0 receives less CPU power
options.C: It may never run again if interactive jobs keep arriving at Q2
options.D: It receives CPU time each time a new job arrives to the system

questions.explanation:
Without priority boost, a CPU-bound job at Q0 can be **starved** indefinitely:

```
Q2  interactive job arrives → runs
Q2  interactive job arrives → runs
Q2  interactive job arrives → runs
Q0  CPU job waits ........... and waits ... and waits
```

If high-priority jobs keep arriving, Q0 never gets CPU time. The **periodic priority boost** (Rule 5) prevents this by moving all jobs back to Q2 every S seconds.

questions.text: Two jobs are both in Q1. Neither has a higher priority than the other. How does MLFQ decide which one runs?

options.A: The one that arrived in the system first gets the CPU
options.B: The one that has used less total CPU time so far gets the CPU
options.C: They take turns using the CPU with a fixed time slice
options.D: The one with the lower process ID number gets the CPU

questions.explanation:
**MLFQ Rule 2**: jobs at the *same* priority run **Round Robin**.

```
Q1: [Job X] [Job Y]
     ──── take turns ────
     X gets slice → Y gets slice → X → Y → ...
```

Within a queue, all jobs are equal — neither arrival order, CPU usage history, nor PID determines priority. They share the CPU fairly via time slices.

questions.text: Which statements about MLFQ are correct?

options.A: Jobs that do frequent I/O tend to stay at higher priority queues
options.B: All jobs always stay in the same queue for their entire time in the system
options.C: A periodic priority boost prevents long-running jobs from being starved
options.D: New jobs always enter at the lowest priority queue to wait their turn

questions.explanation:
**A ✓** — I/O-heavy jobs use only a fraction of their allotment → never exhausted → stay at Q2
**C ✓** — Periodic priority boost prevents starvation of long-running Q0 jobs

**B ✗** — Jobs move constantly: CPU jobs sink down, boosts push everyone up
**D ✗** — New jobs enter at the **highest** queue (Rule 3), not the lowest

questions.text: A job at Q2 uses 9ms of CPU, then does I/O. Allotment per level = 15ms. When it returns from I/O, how much allotment remains at Q2?

options.A: 6ms — the OS tracks total CPU used, even across I/O
options.B: 15ms — the allotment fully resets after every I/O call
options.C: 9ms — previous CPU usage is forgotten when I/O starts
options.D: 0ms — any I/O call uses up all remaining allotment

questions.explanation:
Allotment is tracked **across I/O** — no reset on each I/O call:

```
Q2 allotment: 15ms total
  Used: 9ms (before I/O)
  I/O happens...
  Returns to Q2: 15 - 9 = 6ms remaining
```

**(A) 6ms** — If the job uses those 6ms without blocking again, it exhausts the allotment and is demoted to Q1.

This "memory across I/O" rule prevents programs from gaming MLFQ with tiny spurious I/O calls.

questions.text: A new job arrives in MLFQ. It turns out to be very long and CPU-bound. What will MLFQ do with it over time?

options.A: Keep it at the top queue so other jobs can plan around it
options.B: Gradually demote it to lower queues as it uses up each level's allotment
options.C: Move it immediately to the bottom queue since long jobs are not allowed at top
options.D: Terminate it after a fixed time since long jobs are not allowed in MLFQ

questions.explanation:
Every new job enters at **Q2** (highest) — MLFQ assumes it might be short:

```
New job arrives
      │
      ▼
   enters Q2   ← "optimistic assumption: probably short"
      │
   burns allotment?
   ├── No  → stays high (interactive job, correctly identified)
   └── Yes → demoted (CPU-bound, correctly identified over time)
```

This is how MLFQ approximates SJF without needing to know job lengths upfront.

questions.text: Job X runs 1ms then does I/O, repeatedly. Job Y runs non-stop for a long time. After many rounds, where is each job in MLFQ?

options.A: Both at Q0 — all jobs eventually sink to the bottom queue
options.B: X at Q2, Y at Q0 — X never uses its full allotment, Y gets demoted
options.C: Both at Q2 — priority boost keeps resetting everyone to the top
options.D: X at Q0, Y at Q2 — I/O jobs are penalized and CPU jobs are rewarded

questions.explanation:
After many rounds, their steady-state positions diverge:

```
Q2  ████ X ████ X ████ X    ← X never exhausts allotment, stays here
Q1
Q0            Y              ← Y burned full allotment every level, sank here
```

**X stays high** — 1ms CPU bursts never exhaust the allotment.
**Y sinks to bottom** — continuous CPU use, demoted level by level.

**(B)** is the primary answer; **(A)** is also accepted since after enough boosts Y returns to Q2 only to sink again.

questions.text: MLFQ tries to achieve two goals at once. Which are they?

options.A: Good response time for interactive jobs that do frequent I/O
options.B: Give all jobs exactly equal CPU time over any time period
options.C: Maximize the total number of processes that can run at once
options.D: Good turnaround time by running shorter jobs before longer ones

questions.explanation:
MLFQ solves two competing goals at once:

```
Goal 1: Good TURNAROUND (D)
        → run short jobs first (like SJF, but without foreknowledge)
        → CPU-bound jobs sink; short jobs stay high and finish fast

Goal 2: Good RESPONSE TIME (A)
        → interactive/I/O jobs stay at high priority
        → get CPU immediately after each I/O
```

**B ✗** — Equal CPU time is Round Robin's goal, not MLFQ's
**C ✗** — Maximizing simultaneous processes is a memory management concern
